title: The 'switch' Statement
description: The best way to handle many different paths.

slides.title: Too Many Else Ifs? 😵‍💫
slides.content:
## A Cleaner Approach

If you find yourself writing a huge chain of `else if` statements to check the exact same variable against 10 different values... stop! 

There is a cleaner way: the **`switch`** statement.

slides.title: Switching Cases 🎛️
slides.content:
You provide the variable to `switch` on, and then list `case` blocks for each potential value.

Go evaluates top-to-bottom. If a case matches, it runs that block and **automatically stops**.
slides.codeSnippet:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
    os := "macOS"
    
    switch os {
    case "Windows":
        fmt.Println("You use a PC")
    case "macOS":
        fmt.Println("You use a Mac")
    case "Linux":
        fmt.Println("You use Linux")
    default:
        fmt.Println("Unknown OS")
    }
}

quizData.question: In the code above, what happens if 'os' is 'macOS'?

options.text: It prints 'You use a Mac' and then checks if it is 'Linux'
It prints 'You use a Mac' and stops evaluating the rest of the switch statement

quizData.explanation: Unlike C or Java, Go's switch automatically 'breaks' (stops) after a successful match. No manual `break` keyword is needed!

slides.title: The 'default' Case 🛡️
slides.content:
## The Catch-All

At the bottom of a `switch`, you can place a `default:` case.

This is identical to an `else` at the end of an `if` chain. If absolutely none of the `case` values match, the `default` block will execute safely.

quizData.question: The 'default' case is required in every switch statement.
quizData.explanation: It is optional, but highly recommended as it prevents unexpected scenarios from being ignored.

slides.title: Multiple Values per Case 👯
slides.content: Go allows you to group multiple values on a single check using commas!
